nativeRange.habitat: Oceanic, pelagic; commonly found near the surface in open ocean and reef-associated zones at depths of 0–300 m

diet.notes: In the wild, has been documented feeding on scraps from host sharks, parasites, small fishes, and invertebrates. In captivity, has been reported to accept frozen meaty foods and prepared marine flesh; may initially require live saltwater feeder shrimp to encourage feeding.

summary: A circumtropical pelagic species known for its commensal association with sharks and large marine animals. Generally considered suitable only for very large public aquarium displays; has been reported to reach 70 cm and tends to be aggressive toward smaller tankmates.
careNotes:
The Pilotfish (Naucrates ductor) is an oceanic, circumtropical species best known for its semi-obligate commensal relationship with sharks, rays, large bony fishes, and sea turtles; juveniles tend to associate with jellyfish and drifting seaweed. It is generally considered a public-aquarium-only fish and is not typically seen in the home aquarium trade. A minimum tank of approximately 1,360 L (360 US gallons) has been cited, though even this may be marginal for long-term care given the species' active, fast-growing nature.

Temperature disagreement: FishBase wild-habitat modeling records 7.9–28.1°C, reflecting its broad ocean distribution. Aquarium-care sources (Quality Marine, Aquaryus) suggest a narrower kept range of 18–26°C. The values used here (20–26°C) represent the average of the aquarium-specific recommendations and are more relevant to husbandry. pH data from one source (Aquaryus, 5.9–7.8) was rejected as clearly inconsistent with saltwater parameters; standard marine values of 8.1–8.4 from Quality Marine were used.

Size: FishBase records a maximum of 70 cm TL (common length ~40 cm). Aquarium specimens have been reported to grow rapidly and may reach adult size in relatively short order.

Lifespan: Mediterranean research has documented a maximum recorded age of approximately 3 years, suggesting a relatively short natural lifespan.

Diet: Primarily carnivorous — has been known to eat small fishes and invertebrates; in captivity, tends to accept frozen meaty foods once acclimated.

Grouping: Naturally schooling; has been reported to prefer other Pilotfish as tankmates. However, will eat fish smaller than itself, so compatible tankmates are limited to similarly sized or larger fish.

Note: LiveAquaria's product page for 'Pilotfish' lists the scientific name Nathanodon speciosus (a trade synonym for Gnathanodon speciosus / Golden Trevally), which is a different species. This entry is based on Naucrates ductor, the true Pilotfish, as specified by the manifest and confirmed via FishBase ID 998. Husbandry parameters were cross-referenced from Quality Marine (which correctly identifies Naucrates ductor) and FishBase.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity has not been documented in the available literature. This species is regarded as practically impossible to breed in home aquariums; public aquaria have focused on display keeping rather than breeding programs.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: LiveAquaria's 'Pilotfish' product page (https://www.liveaquaria.com/products/pilotfish) lists the scientific name Nathanodon speciosus (Gnathanodon speciosus / Golden Trevally), which is a different species from Naucrates ductor. The collection page was used as the primary source root per manifest; husbandry data was sourced from Quality Marine and FishBase which correctly identify Naucrates ductor.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed species ID 998 = Naucrates ductor (Linnaeus, 1758). Provided taxonomy, max size (70 cm), circumtropical distribution, temperature range (wild: 7.9–28.1°C), diet, and behavior notes.

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Correctly identifies Naucrates ductor. Provided tank size (360 gal / ~1362 L), salinity (SG 1.020–1.025), temperature (72–78°F = 22.2–25.6°C), pH (8.1–8.4), dKH (8–12), care level (moderate to difficult), and temperament (aggressive). Used as primary cross-verification source.

additional.name: Aquaryus
additional.notes: Provided temperature range 18–26°C and size 70 cm. pH data (5.9–7.8) and tank size (420 L) from this source were rejected as inconsistent with known saltwater parameters and other sources. Temperature values were averaged with Quality Marine.

fish.breedingNotes: No documented captive breeding. This species is kept only in large public aquarium exhibits.
